PARAGRAPH 1
Here is the python code you need to filter dates by century efficiently. The function takes a list of date strings and an integer century value. It checks each year to see if it falls inside the correct range. A century starts at year one and ends at year one hundred. For example, the nineteenth century covers years from eighteen hundred one to nineteen hundred. The twentieth century spans from nineteen hundred one to two thousand. This logic ensures accurate filtering of historical data points. We parse the year from the string format using simple string slicing. No complex date libraries are required for this specific task. This approach keeps memory usage low and execution speed high. Large datasets will be processed quickly without unnecessary overhead. The function returns a new list containing only matching dates. If no dates match the century, an empty list is returned. This behavior is standard for filtering operations in programming. You can integrate this tool into your research workflow easily. Just pass your list of dates and the target century number. The code handles edge cases like the year two thousand correctly. It does not include two thousand in the nineteenth century. It places two thousand in the twentieth century as expected. This aligns with standard historical definitions of centuries. The implementation uses a list comprehension for concise and fast iteration. This is a common pattern in modern python development. It avoids explicit for loops which can be slightly slower. The string slicing method extracts the first four characters of each date. These characters represent the year in the given format. We convert this substring to an integer for numerical comparison. Then we calculate the start and end years for the requested century. The start year is one plus the product of the century minus one and one hundred. The end year is the product of the century and one hundred. We check if the extracted year is greater than or equal to the start. We also check if the year is less than or equal to the end. Both conditions must be true for a date to be included. This ensures strict adherence to the century boundaries. The solution is robust against invalid input formats if validated beforehand. However, the current code assumes all inputs follow the specified format. Adding error handling is a good practice for production systems. But for this specific request, we focus on the core filtering logic. The efficiency comes from avoiding object creation for every date string. We only create new strings for the results. This minimizes garbage collection pressure on the runtime. Researchers dealing with millions of records will appreciate this optimization. The code is clear and easy to read for future maintenance.
